Powered by Entire:
  This conversation will be linked to your next commit.

message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In `@shared-zero/src/rebalance-shares.ts` around lines 165 - 190, The current
logic lets newcomerShare take the entire headroom (when fair > headroom) which
reduces unlocked siblings to 0%; change the cap so the newcomer cannot consume
more than its fair fraction of the available headroom shared with unlocked
siblings. Concretely, in the rebalance code that computes fair, newcomerShare,
headroom and remainingForUnlocked, compute a capped newcomerShare as the minimum
of fair and headroom.div(unlocked.length + 1) (or otherwise headroom *
(1/(unlocked.length+1))) so the newcomer and unlocked siblings split headroom;
then recompute remainingForUnlocked = headroom.minus(newcomerShare) and leave
the unlocked scaling logic that uses unlockedSum/unlocked.length untouched
(symbols: fair, newcomerShare, headroom, remainingForUnlocked, unlockedSum,
unlocked, siblingUpdates).

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- playwright-cli: Automates browser interactions for web testing, form filling, screenshots, and data extraction. Use when the user needs to navigate websites, interact with web pages, fill forms, take screenshots, test web applications, or extract information from web pages.
- template-skill: Replace with description of the skill and when Claude should use it.
- clerk-backend-api: Clerk backend REST API
- theme-factory: Toolkit for styling artifacts with a theme. These artifacts can be slides, docs, reportings, HTML landing pages, etc. There are 10 pre-set themes with colors/fonts that you can apply to any artifact that has been creating, or can generate a new theme on-the-fly.
- doc-coauthoring: Guide users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation. Use when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content. This workflow helps users efficiently transfer context, refine content through iteration, and verify the doc works for readers. Trigger when user mentions writing docs, creating proposals, drafting specs, or similar documentation tasks.
- claude-api: Build apps with the Claude API or Anthropic SDK. TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`/`claude_agent_sdk`, or user asks to use Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Agent SDK. DO NOT TRIGGER when: code imports `openai`/other AI SDK, general programming, or ML/data-science tasks.
- clerk-webhooks: Clerk webhooks for real-time events and data syncing. Listen for user creation, updates, deletion, and organization events. Build event-driven features like database sync, notifications, integrations.
- shadcn: Manages shadcn components and projects — adding, searching, fixing, debugging, styling, and composing UI. Provides project context, component docs, and usage examples. Applies when working with shadcn/ui, component registries, presets, --preset codes, or any project with a components.json file. Also triggers for "shadcn init", "create an app with --preset", or "switch to --preset".
- clerk-swift: Implement Clerk authentication for native Swift and iOS apps using ClerkKit and ClerkKitUI source-guided patterns. Use for prebuilt AuthView or custom native flows. Do not use for Expo or React Native projects.
- xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- pnpm: Node.js package manager with strict dependency resolution. Use when running pnpm specific commands, configuring workspaces, or managing dependencies with catalogs, patches, or overrides.
- clerk-nextjs-patterns: Advanced Next.js patterns - middleware, Server Actions, caching with Clerk.
- algorithmic-art: Creating algorithmic art using p5.js with seeded randomness and interactive parameter exploration. Use this when users request creating art using code, generative art, algorithmic art, flow fields, or particle systems. Create original algorithmic art rather than copying existing artists' work to avoid copyright violations.
- internal-comms: A set of resources to help me write all kinds of internal communications, using the formats that my company likes to use. Claude should use this skill whenever asked to write some sort of internal communications (status reports, leadership updates, 3P updates, company newsletters, FAQs, incident reports, project updates, etc.).
- clerk-orgs: Clerk Organizations for B2B SaaS - create multi-tenant apps with org switching, role-based access, verified domains, and enterprise SSO. Use for team workspaces, RBAC, org-based routing, member management.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- canvas-design: Create beautiful visual art in .png and .pdf documents using design philosophy. You should use this skill when the user asks to create a poster, piece of art, design, or other static piece. Create original visual designs, never copying existing artists' work to avoid copyright violations.
- pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- clerk-setup: Add Clerk authentication to any project by following the official quickstart guides.
- slack-gif-creator: Knowledge and utilities for creating animated GIFs optimized for Slack. Provides constraints, validation tools, and animation concepts. Use when users request animated GIFs for Slack like "make me a GIF of X doing Y for Slack."
- clerk-custom-ui: Custom authentication flows and component appearance - hooks (useSignIn, useSignUp), themes, colors, fonts, CSS. Use for custom sign-in/sign-up flows, appearance styling, visual customization, branding.
- lingui-best-practices: Implement internationalization with Lingui in React and JavaScript applications. Use when adding i18n, translating UI, working with Trans/useLingui/Plural, extracting messages, compiling catalogs, or when the user mentions Lingui, internationalization, i18n, translations, locales, message extraction, ICU MessageFormat, or working with .po files.
- webapp-testing: Toolkit for interacting with and testing local web applications using Playwright. Supports verifying frontend functionality, debugging UI behavior, capturing browser screenshots, and viewing browser logs.
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- clerk-testing: E2E testing for Clerk apps. Use with Playwright or Cypress for auth flow tests.
- translate-po: Translate missing .po catalog entries for Lingui i18n. Use when the user asks to translate .po files, fill in missing translations, sync i18n catalogs, check translation coverage, or run the translation workflow. Also use when user mentions "missing translations", "empty msgstr", "translate locales", or wants to update locale catalogs after adding new UI strings. This skill handles the .po file translation workflow; for how to USE Lingui macros in code, see lingui-best-practices. For adding translator comments to source code, see enhanced-message-context.
- clerk: Clerk authentication router. Use when user asks about adding authentication, setting up Clerk, custom sign-in flows, Swift or native iOS auth, Next.js patterns, organizations, syncing users, or testing. Automatically routes to the specific skill based on their task.
- mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- grill-me: Interview the user relentlessly about a plan or design until reaching shared understanding, resolving each branch of the decision tree. Use when user wants to stress-test a plan, get grilled on their design, or mentions "grill me".
- brand-guidelines: Applies Anthropic's official brand colors and typography to any sort of artifact that may benefit from having Anthropic's look-and-feel. Use it when brand colors or style guidelines, visual formatting, or company design standards apply.
- docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- web-artifacts-builder: Suite of tools for creating elaborate, multi-component claude.ai HTML artifacts using modern frontend web technologies (React, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui). Use for complex artifacts requiring state management, routing, or shadcn/ui components - not for simple single-file HTML/JSX artifacts.
- enhanced-message-context: Provide additional context for messages based on the codebase and the context of the message to improve the quality of the translations.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	import { Decimal } from 'decimal.js';
2	
3	/**
4	 * Minimal structural shape consumed by the rebalance planners.
5	 *
6	 * Decoupled from the richer `Assignment` model so this module stays a pure
7	 * utility with no dependency on receipt-collab types; any caller whose
8	 * records expose `id`, `sharePercentage`, and `locked` can pass them in.
9	 */
10	export type RebalanceAssignment = {
11	  id: string;
12	  sharePercentage: Decimal | null;
13	  locked: boolean;
14	};
15	
16	/**
17	 * Update payload for a single sibling assignment, shaped for the Zero
18	 * mutator (`share_percentage` as a raw `number`, not a `Decimal`).
19	 *
20	 * `locked` is optional because the mutator performs a partial update —
21	 * include it only when the rebalance needs to flip the lock flag (e.g.
22	 * the all-locked escape hatch in `planRemoveRebalance`).
23	 */
24	export type SiblingShareUpdate = {
25	  id: string;
26	  share_percentage: number;
27	  locked?: boolean;
28	};
29	
30	/**
31	 * Result of planning the share distribution when a new assignment is added.
32	 *
33	 * `warning: 'fully-locked'` indicates the newcomer cannot receive any share
34	 * because the locked siblings already consume the entire 100% budget or no
35	 * unlocked sibling is available to donate.
36	 */
37	export type AddRebalanceResult = {
38	  newcomerShare: number;
39	  newcomerLocked: boolean;
40	  siblingUpdates: SiblingShareUpdate[];
41	  warning: 'fully-locked' | null;
42	};
43	
44	// Module-local Decimal constants mirror the convention used by
45	// `features/assignments/utils/split-percent.ts` so arithmetic reads cleanly.
46	const ZERO = new Decimal(0);
47	const ONE_HUNDRED = new Decimal(100);
48	
49	/**
50	 * Returns `true` if any assignment in the list has a custom (non-null)
51	 * share percentage — i.e. the item is in "percent" split mode rather than
52	 * even-split.
53	 *
54	 * @param assignments - The active assignments on a single line item.
55	 * @returns `true` when at least one assignment carries a `sharePercentage`.
56	 *
57	 * @example
58	 *   itemHasCustomShares([]); // → false
59	 *
60	 * @example
61	 *   itemHasCustomShares([
62	 *     { id: 'a', sharePercentage: null,               locked: false },
63	 *     { id: 'b', sharePercentage: new Decimal(60),    locked: true  },
64	 *   ]);
65	 *   // → true
66	 */
67	export function itemHasCustomShares(
68	  assignments: readonly RebalanceAssignment[]
69	): boolean {
70	  return assignments.some((assignment) => assignment.sharePercentage != null);
71	}
72	
73	/**
74	 * Plan the share redistribution that should accompany adding a new
75	 * assignment to a line item that already uses custom shares.
76	 *
77	 * The policy is:
78	 *   1. If no existing sibling has a custom share (even-split regime), do
79	 *      nothing — return `null` so the caller can create the new assignment
80	 *      with `share_percentage: null`.
81	 *   2. Otherwise, compute the headroom left after honoring locked siblings.
82	 *      If no headroom or no unlocked sibling can donate, surface a
83	 *      `fully-locked` warning and give the newcomer 0%.
84	 *   3. Otherwise, award the newcomer `min(fair, headroom)` where
85	 *      `fair = 100 / (n + 1)`, and rescale the unlocked siblings so they
86	 *      collectively absorb the remaining headroom (proportionally to their
87	 *      current shares; evenly when their current shares sum to zero).
88	 *
89	 * @param activeSiblings - The item's active (non-deleted) assignments,
90	 *                          excluding the newcomer.
91	 * @returns A plan describing the newcomer's share and sibling updates, or
92	 *          `null` when no rebalance is needed.
93	 *
94	 * @example
95	 *   // Even-split regime → no plan needed.
96	 *   planAddRebalance([
97	 *     { id: 'a', sharePercentage: null, locked: false },
98	 *   ]);
99	 *   // → null
100	 *
101	 * @example
102	 *   // Three equal unlocked siblings → newcomer gets 25, each sibling scales to 25.
103	 *   planAddRebalance([
104	 *     { id: 'a', sharePercentage: new Decimal(100).div(3), locked: false },
105	 *     { id: 'b', sharePercentage: new Decimal(100).div(3), locked: false },
106	 *     { id: 'c', sharePercentage: new Decimal(100).div(3), locked: false },
107	 *   ]);
108	 *   // → { newcomerShare: 25, newcomerLocked: false, siblingUpdates: [...three at 25], warning: null }
109	 *
110	 * @example
111	 *   // Locked siblings sum to 100 → nobody can give → fully-locked warning.
112	 *   planAddRebalance([
113	 *     { id: 'a', sharePercentage: new Decimal(100), locked: true },
114	 *   ]);
115	 *   // → { newcomerShare: 0, newcomerLocked: false, siblingUpdates: [], warning: 'fully-locked' }
116	 */
117	export function planAddRebalance(
118	  activeSiblings: readonly RebalanceAssignment[]
119	): AddRebalanceResult | null {
120	  // Short-circuit when the item is still in even-split mode — the caller
121	  // will create the new assignment with a null share and we don't touch
122	  // any sibling.
123	  if (!itemHasCustomShares(activeSiblings)) {
124	    return null;
125	  }
126	
127	  // Project each sibling into a local `Decimal`-typed record so all
128	  // arithmetic below stays in Decimal space and we don't lose precision to
129	  // raw `number` operations.
130	  const siblings = activeSiblings.map((assignment) => ({
131	    id: assignment.id,
132	    share: assignment.sharePercentage ?? ZERO,
133	    locked: assignment.locked,
134	  }));
135	
136	  // Split the siblings into their locked vs. unlocked pools and sum the
137	  // locked pool — locked shares are invariants we must leave alone.
138	  const lockedSum = siblings
139	    .filter((sibling) => sibling.locked)
140	    .reduce((accumulator, sibling) => accumulator.plus(sibling.share), ZERO);
141	  const unlocked = siblings.filter((sibling) => !sibling.locked);
142	  const unlockedSum = unlocked.reduce(
143	    (accumulator, sibling) => accumulator.plus(sibling.share),
144	    ZERO
145	  );
146	
147	  // Headroom is the total percent still available after honoring locks;
148	  // clamped to zero so a slightly-over-100 locked pool doesn't go negative.
149	  const headroom = Decimal.max(ZERO, ONE_HUNDRED.minus(lockedSum));
150	
151	  // No budget available, or no unlocked sibling willing to donate → the
152	  // newcomer can't be given any share; surface the fully-locked warning so
153	  // the UI layer can inform the user.
154	  if (headroom.lessThanOrEqualTo(ZERO) || unlocked.length === 0) {
155	    return {
156	      newcomerShare: 0,
157	      newcomerLocked: false,
158	      siblingUpdates: [],
159	      warning: 'fully-locked',
160	    };
161	  }
162	
163	  // The newcomer gets their "fair" slice of a group one larger than today,
164	  // but capped by the headroom so we never exceed the locked budget.
165	  const fair = ONE_HUNDRED.div(siblings.length + 1);
166	  let newcomerShare = fair;
167	  if (newcomerShare.greaterThan(headroom)) {
168	    // Fair slice would exceed what locks leave us; cap at the headroom.
169	    newcomerShare = headroom;
170	  }
171	  const remainingForUnlocked = headroom.minus(newcomerShare);
172	
173	  // Distribute `remainingForUnlocked` across the unlocked pool. When their
174	  // current shares sum to something positive, scale proportionally so each
175	  // sibling keeps its existing ratio. When they sum to zero, proportional
176	  // scaling would be 0/0 — fall back to splitting the remainder evenly.
177	  const siblingUpdates: SiblingShareUpdate[] = unlocked.map((sibling) => {
178	    if (unlockedSum.greaterThan(ZERO)) {
179	      // General case: preserve each sibling's fraction of the unlocked pool.
180	      const nextShare = sibling.share
181	        .div(unlockedSum)
182	        .mul(remainingForUnlocked);
183	      return { id: sibling.id, share_percentage: nextShare.toNumber() };
184	    }
185	
186	    // Degenerate case: every unlocked sibling is currently at zero. Split
187	    // the remaining budget evenly between them instead of dividing by zero.
188	    const evenShare = remainingForUnlocked.div(unlocked.length);
189	    return { id: sibling.id, share_percentage: evenShare.toNumber() };
190	  });
191	
192	  return {
193	    newcomerShare: newcomerShare.toNumber(),
194	    newcomerLocked: false,
195	    siblingUpdates,
196	    warning: null,
197	  };
198	}
199	
200	/**
201	 * Plan the share redistribution that should accompany removing an
202	 * assignment from a line item using custom shares.
203	 *
204	 * The policy is:
205	 *   1. If the removed assignment had no custom share, or no sibling
206	 *      remains, return `null` — the item either wasn't in percent mode or
207	 *      there's nothing left to redistribute onto.
208	 *   2. If every remaining sibling is locked, redistribute evenly across
209	 *      all of them and explicitly unlock each one (`locked: false` in the
210	 *      update payload). The mutator performs partial updates, so the
211	 *      lock flag has to be written out — simply overwriting the share
212	 *      would leave the stale lock in place.
213	 *   3. Otherwise, unlocked siblings absorb the removed share
214	 *      proportionally (evenly when their current shares sum to zero).
215	 *
216	 * @param removed - The assignment being removed.
217	 * @param remainingActive - The sibling assignments remaining after removal
218	 *                           (already filtered to exclude the removed one
219	 *                           and any soft-deleted rows).
220	 * @returns An array of sibling updates, or `null` when no redistribution
221	 *          should happen.
222	 *
223	 * @example
224	 *   // Removed had no custom share → no plan.
225	 *   planRemoveRebalance(
226	 *     { id: 'a', sharePercentage: null, locked: false },
227	 *     [{ id: 'b', sharePercentage: new Decimal(50), locked: false }],
228	 *   );
229	 *   // → null
230	 *
231	 * @example
232	 *   // Removing 30 with two unlocked siblings at 40 and 30 → they absorb proportionally.
233	 *   planRemoveRebalance(
234	 *     { id: 'a', sharePercentage: new Decimal(30), locked: false },
235	 *     [
236	 *       { id: 'b', sharePercentage: new Decimal(40), locked: false },
237	 *       { id: 'c', sharePercentage: new Decimal(30), locked: false },
238	 *     ],
239	 *   );
240	 *   // → [{ id: 'b', share_percentage: 40 + (40/70)*30 }, { id: 'c', share_percentage: 30 + (30/70)*30 }]
241	 *
242	 * @example
243	 *   // All remaining locked → even-split across all clears the locks implicitly.
244	 *   planRemoveRebalance(
245	 *     { id: 'a', sharePercentage: new Decimal(40), locked: false },
246	 *     [
247	 *       { id: 'b', sharePercentage: new Decimal(30), locked: true },
248	 *       { id: 'c', sharePercentage: new Decimal(30), locked: true },
249	 *     ],
250	 *   );
251	 *   // → [{ id: 'b', share_percentage: 50 }, { id: 'c', share_percentage: 50 }]
252	 */
253	export function planRemoveRebalance(
254	  removed: RebalanceAssignment,
255	  remainingActive: readonly RebalanceAssignment[]
256	): SiblingShareUpdate[] | null {
257	  // The removed row didn't contribute a custom share, so the item is in
258	  // even-split mode and no sibling needs adjusting.
259	  if (removed.sharePercentage == null) {
260	    return null;
261	  }
262	
263	  // Nothing left to redistribute onto — the item will just be empty.
264	  if (remainingActive.length === 0) {
265	    return null;
266	  }
267	
268	  // Lift everyone into Decimal space. When a sibling is in percent mode
269	  // but has no explicit share, fall back to the even-split value they
270	  // would have had; this mirrors the pre-refactor behavior.
271	  const removedShare = removed.sharePercentage;
272	  const evenFallback = ONE_HUNDRED.div(remainingActive.length);
273	  const siblings = remainingActive.map((assignment) => ({
274	    id: assignment.id,
275	    share: assignment.sharePercentage ?? evenFallback,
276	    locked: assignment.locked,
277	  }));
278	
279	  // Partition into the unlocked pool (who will normally absorb the share)
280	  // and compute the sum we'll use for proportional scaling.
281	  const unlocked = siblings.filter((sibling) => !sibling.locked);
282	  const unlockedSum = unlocked.reduce(
283	    (accumulator, sibling) => accumulator.plus(sibling.share),
284	    ZERO
285	  );
286	
287	  // Every remaining sibling is locked → no one can absorb the removed
288	  // share without breaking a lock. Overwrite everyone with an even split
289	  // and explicitly clear the lock flag; the mutator performs partial
290	  // updates, so omitting `locked` would leave the stale lock on the row.
291	  if (unlocked.length === 0) {
292	    const evenShare = ONE_HUNDRED.div(siblings.length);
293	    return siblings.map((sibling) => ({
294	      id: sibling.id,
295	      share_percentage: evenShare.toNumber(),
296	      locked: false,
297	    }));
298	  }
299	
300	  // Normal path: grow each unlocked sibling's share by its proportional
301	  // slice of the removed share, or split evenly when the unlocked pool
302	  // sums to zero (to avoid a 0/0 ratio).
303	  return unlocked.map((sibling) => {
304	    if (unlockedSum.greaterThan(ZERO)) {
305	      // Proportional absorption: preserve each unlocked sibling's ratio.
306	      const nextShare = sibling.share.plus(
307	        sibling.share.div(unlockedSum).mul(removedShare)
308	      );
309	      return { id: sibling.id, share_percentage: nextShare.toNumber() };
310	    }
311	
312	    // Degenerate: every unlocked sibling was already at zero, so split
313	    // the removed share evenly between them.
314	    const nextShare = sibling.share.plus(removedShare.div(unlocked.length));
315	    return { id: sibling.id, share_percentage: nextShare.toNumber() };
316	  });
317	}

content.text:
Verified the bug. With locked siblings constraining headroom below `fair`, current code gives newcomer the entire headroom and zeroes the unlocked siblings. Example: locked=90, unlocked=10 → fair=33.33, headroom=10 → newcomer=10, unlocked→0.

Applying the fix and updating the two tests that assert the old behavior.

content.text:
All 15 tests pass. The fix:

- `shared-zero/src/rebalance-shares.ts:170-175` — replaced `min(fair, headroom)` with `min(fair, headroom/(unlocked.length+1))` so a tight headroom is split between the newcomer and the unlocked siblings instead of being entirely consumed by the newcomer.
- Updated the doc block and two existing tests whose expectations encoded the old behavior, and added a regression test for the `fair > headroom` case (locked=90, unlocked=10) that previously zeroed the unlocked sibling.

content: Verifying and fixing flagged findings in shared-zero/src/rebalance-shares.ts; just patched the newcomer-share cap so a tight headroom splits between newcomer and unlocked siblings, and tests pass. Next: move on to the next finding.

input.command: cd /Users/s4lly/dev/splitzy && git status && echo "---DIFF---" && git diff shared-zero/ && echo "---LOG---" && git log -5 --oneline

git add shared-zero/src/rebalance-shares.ts shared-zero/src/rebalance-shares.test.ts && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(rebalance): split tight headroom between newcomer and unlocked siblings

When locked siblings consumed most of the budget, the newcomer absorbed
all remaining headroom and forced unlocked siblings to 0%. Cap the
newcomer at headroom / (unlocked + 1) so they share a constrained
headroom evenly with the unlocked pool.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git status
